name: Lever Switch
description: An interactable switch changes state and drives linked targets such as doors, platforms, hazards, or logic nodes.
design_notes: Lever switches are readable when switch_state, linked_target_ids, debounce, and reset boundaries are explicit. Level designers need to know whether the lever is momentary, latched, timed, reversible, saved permanently, or part of a larger dependency graph.
design_purpose: Give players a simple world interaction that visibly changes connected puzzle state.
Support cause-and-effect learning through doors, lights, platforms, traps, or multi-switch logic.
Create compact puzzle graphs that can be reset, saved, and debugged reliably.
player_fantasy: The player feels they have manipulated the room and can read what changed because of their action.

parameters.name: interactable_id
parameters.description: Stable switch identifier used by interaction, save state, and debug visualization.

parameters.name: switch_state
parameters.description: Authoritative state used by animation, linked targets, and persistence.

parameters.name: linked_target_ids
parameters.description: Targets that receive switch-state changes through ordered puzzle events.

parameters.name: debounce_window_ms
parameters.description: Minimum interval before repeated interaction requests may change the switch again.

parameters.name: reset_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that restores or preserves switch state across resets and reloads.

edge_cases: A switch is pulled while linked door animation is still moving from the previous state.
Multiple switches control one target and their dependency graph reaches a contradictory state.
A player saves after the switch changed but before all target events finished.
In multiplayer, two players interact with the same switch within one debounce window.
common_bugs: Lever animation changes locally but linked target state is rejected by authority validation.
Switch state resets visually after load while the door remains open.
Events fire in nondeterministic order and multi-switch puzzles become unsolvable after repeated toggles.
A disabled switch still accepts buffered interaction input.
balancing_notes: Players should be able to see, hear, or infer at least one linked target when learning a new switch rule.
Timed return switches should give enough margin for route reading, not only perfect execution.
Complex switch networks need hints or spatial layout that makes the dependency graph learnable.
accessibility_notes: Use persistent visual position, text prompts, and audio cues to show switch state.
Avoid making critical switches distinguishable only by small red/green light changes.

implementation_notes.unity: Model the lever as a state component with interactable_id, switch_state, debounce_window_ms, and linked_target_ids. Dispatch target changes from the committed state, then let Animator and audio observe state instead of owning it.
implementation_notes.godot: Use a node with exported target ids and a puzzle state resource for persistence. Signals should carry switch_state and puzzle_step_index so linked nodes can apply events in stable order.
implementation_notes.unreal: An Actor Component can own switch state and target references, with server RPCs validating interaction distance and debounce in multiplayer. Replicate switch_state and drive lever mesh, sounds, and target actors from OnRep handlers.
implementation_notes.web: Keep switch state in the puzzle model and render lever pose from that model. Canvas or Phaser interactions should enqueue a single toggle request and ignore extra clicks until the committed state version changes.

example_games.title: Portal 2
example_games.note: Uses switches and linked targets in readable puzzle rooms.

example_games.title: The Talos Principle
example_games.note: Uses switch-like interactables as part of room logic.
